Bar Sinister

Breakfast in Pond Cottage on that Sunday morning proved to be the pleasantest meal that Wolf had yet enjoyed under the Otter roof.

Mrs.¬†Otter, dressed in stiff puce-coloured silk, and happy to have both her sons at the table, spoke at some length to their guest about the morning service in the church to which she and Darnley were presently to go. She explained to him how much she liked the quiet, reverent manner in which Mr.¬†Valley conducted the worship of the parish.

‚ÄúHe makes me sad at other times,‚ÄĚ she said. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs an unhappy little man; and everyone knows how he drinks. He ought to have a wife to look after him, or at least a housekeeper. He‚Äôs got no one in the house. How he gets enough to eat I can‚Äôt imagine.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúMother thinks no household can get on for a day without a woman in it,‚ÄĚ said Darnley.

Jason Otter‚Äôs pallid face reddened a little. ‚ÄúOf course, we know he wants to be the only man that any of the village-boys admire. It‚Äôs human nature‚Ā†‚ÄĒthat‚Äôs what it is. These country clergymen are all the same.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThere are the bells!‚ÄĚ cried Mrs.¬†Otter, thankful for the opportunity of staving off discord between the brothers. They all four listened in silence, while the faint notes from the Henry the Seventh tower penetrated the walls of Pond Cottage.

‚ÄúThat means it‚Äôs ten o‚Äôclock,‚ÄĚ said Darnley. ‚ÄúThey ring again at half-past, don‚Äôt they, Mother?‚ÄĚ

Wolf felt an extraordinary sense of peacefulness in the air that morning. The sound of the bells accentuated it; and he wondered vaguely to himself whether he wouldn‚Äôt offer to go to church with the mother and the son.

‚ÄúBy the way,‚ÄĚ he remarked, ‚Äúmay I ask you people a question, while I think of it?‚ÄĚ

They all three awoke from their individual meditations and gave him their undivided attention. Mrs.¬†Otter did this with serene complacency, evidently assuming that the nature of his remark would prove harmless and agreeable. Jason did it with nervous concern, touched with a flicker of what looked like personal fear. Darnley did it with an expression of weary politeness, as much as to say, ‚ÄúOh, God! Oh, God! Am I not going to have even Sunday free from other people‚Äôs problems?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs a simple enough thing,‚ÄĚ Wolf said quickly, realizing that he had made more stir than he intended. ‚ÄúI only wanted to know why this house of yours is called Pond Cottage, when there‚Äôs no trace of a pond.‚ÄĚ

There was an instantaneous sign of startled agitation all the way round the table.

‚ÄúThe pond is there all right,‚ÄĚ said Darnley, quietly. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs over that hedge, just outside our gate, the other side of the lane. It‚Äôs rather an uncomfortable topic with us, Solent; because at least three times James Redfern thought of drowning himself in it. He may have thought of it more times than that. Jason found him there three times. We don‚Äôt like the pond for that reason. That‚Äôs all!‚ÄĚ

Jason Otter got up from his chair. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll go and put on my boots,‚ÄĚ he remarked to Wolf, ‚Äúand we‚Äôll go and visit the pond. You ought to see it. And there are other things I can show you, too, while mother and Darnley are in church. You‚Äôve got your boots on, I think? Well! I won‚Äôt keep you very long.‚ÄĚ

He left the room as he spoke and Mrs.¬†Otter looked appealingly at her younger son.

‚ÄúDon‚Äôt worry, Mother dear,‚ÄĚ said Darnley, gravely, laying his hand upon her knees.

He turned to Wolf. ‚ÄúYou must help us in keeping my brother in good spirits, Solent,‚ÄĚ he said. ‚ÄúBut I know I can trust you.‚ÄĚ

When Wolf and Jason did finally cross the lane together and enter the opposite field‚Ā†‚ÄĒwhich they achieved by climbing up a steep bank and pushing their way through a gap in the hedge‚Ā†‚ÄĒthe sense of peacefulness in the whole air of the place had intensified to a degree that was so enchanting to Wolf that nothing seemed able to disturb his contentment.

The field he found himself in was a very large one, and only a broken, wavering line of willows and poplars at the further end of it gave any indication of the presence of water. The atmosphere was deliciously hushed and misty; no wind was stirring; and the placid morning sun fell upon the grass and the trees with a sort of largeness of indifference, as if it were too happy, in some secretive way of its own, to care whether its warmth gave pleasure or the reverse to the lives that thrived under its influence. It seemed to possess the secret of complete detachment, this sunshine; but it seemed also to possess the secret of projecting the clue to such detachment into the heart of every living existence that its vaporous warmth approached.

Wolf was suddenly aware of a rising to the surface of his mind of that trance-like ‚Äúmythology‚ÄĚ of his. All the little outward things that met his gaze seemed to form so many material moulds into which this magnetic current set itself to run.

He surveyed a patch of sun-dried cattle-dung upon which the abstracted Jason had inadvertently planted his foot and across which was slowly moving with exquisite precaution a brilliantly green beetle. He surveyed a group of small crimson-topped daisies over which a sturdy, flowerless thistle threw a faint and patient shadow. He surveyed the disordered flight of a flock of starlings, heading away from the pond towards the village. But of all these things what arrested him most was the least obvious, the least noticeable. It was, in fact, no more than a certain ridge of rough unevenness in the ground at his feet; a nameless unevenness, which assumed, as the misty sunlight wavered over it, the predominant place in this accidental pattern of impressions.

Jason said nothing at all as they walked together slowly across the field. The man had ostentatiously avoided any approach to Sunday clothes that morning; and, without hat or stick, in a very shabby overcoat, he presented rather a lamentable figure, as he led the way forward towards Lenty Pond.

They reached the willows and poplars at last; and Wolf stared in astonishment at what he saw. He found himself standing on the brink of an expanse of water that was nearly as large as a small lake. The opposite side of it was entirely covered with a bed of thick reeds, among which he could see the little red-and-black shapes of several moorhens moving; but from where he stood, under these willows, right away to the pond‚Äôs centre, the water was deep and dark, and even on that placid Sunday a little menacing.

‚ÄúHe could have done it easily if he‚Äôd wanted to, couldn‚Äôt he?‚ÄĚ said Jason, gazing at the water. ‚ÄúThe truth is he didn‚Äôt want to! Darnley‚Äôs a sentimental fool. Redfern didn‚Äôt want to drown himself. Not a bit of it. What did he come here for, then? He came to rouse pity, to make people‚Äôs minds go crazy with pity.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThe man must have been thinking of saying just this to me all the way across the field,‚ÄĚ thought Wolf. But Jason jerked out now a much more disturbing sentence.

‚ÄúThe boy did upset one person‚Äôs mind. He made one person‚Äôs mind feel like a weed in this water! And you‚Äôd be surprised to hear who that person was.‚ÄĚ

But Wolf just then felt it very hard to give him his complete attention. For although the mystical ecstasy he had just experienced had faded, everything about the day had become momentous in his hidden secretive life; and he felt detached, remote, disembodied, for all his Sunday clothes. He could hear the cawing of a couple of rooks high up in the sky; and even when they ceased cawing, the creaking of their wings seemed like the indolence of the very day itself. ‚ÄúA weed in the water,‚ÄĚ he echoed mechanically; while his mind, voyaging over those hushed West Country pastures, followed the creaking wings.

‚ÄúWho was it, Mr.¬†Otter, who was so upset by Redfern?‚ÄĚ

The appeal in Jason‚Äôs miserable eyes grew still more disturbing. The man‚Äôs soul seemed to come waveringly forward, like a grey vapour, out of its eye-sockets, till it formed itself into a shadowy double of the person who stood by Wolf‚Äôs side.

‚ÄúCan‚Äôt you guess?‚ÄĚ murmured Jason Otter. ‚ÄúIt was I‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ I‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ I‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ You‚Äôre surprised. Well, anyone would be. You wouldn‚Äôt have thought of that, though you are Mr.¬†Urquhart‚Äôs secretary and have come from a college! But you needn‚Äôt look like that; for it‚Äôs true! Darnley sentimentalizes about his death, which was unfortunate, of course, but perfectly natural‚Ā†‚ÄĒhe died of pneumonia, as any of us might!‚Ā†‚ÄĒbut what drove me to distraction was this playing upon a person‚Äôs pity. He always did it‚Ā†‚ÄĒfrom the very first day. Darnley yielded to it at once, though he never liked the boy. I resisted it. I am of iron in these things. I know too much. But by degrees, can‚Äôt you understand, though I didn‚Äôt yield to it, it began to bother my mind. Pity‚Äôs the most cruel trap ever invented. You can see that, I suppose? Take it that there were only one unhappy person left, why, it might spoil all the delight in the world! That is why I‚Äôd like to kill pity‚Ā†‚ÄĒwhy I‚Äôd like to make people see what madness it is.‚ÄĚ

Wolf drew away from him a step or two, till he stood at the very edge of the pond, and then he remarked abruptly, ‚ÄúYour mother told me that Redfern was one of the most good-looking young men she has ever seen.‚ÄĚ

Having flung out these words, he began flicking the dark, brimming water with the end of his stick, watching the ripples which he caused spreading far out towards the centre. Exactly why he made that remark just then he would have found it hard to explain. The wraithlike phantom-soul that had emerged from Jason‚Äôs eye-sockets drew back instantaneously, like a puppet pulled by a string; and over the two apertures into which it withdrew there formed a glacial film of guarded suspicion.

‚ÄúI have seen better-looking ones,‚ÄĚ said Jason Otter drily. ‚ÄúHe used to help that fool Valley in his High Church services. I don‚Äôt know whether the Virgin Mary ever appeared to him; but I know he used to take her flowers, because he used to steal them out of our garden! My mother let him steal because it was‚Ā†‚ÄĒHullo! What‚Äôs up now? Who‚Äôs this?‚ÄĚ

Wolf swung round and observed to his surprise the tall figure of Roger Monk advancing towards them across the field.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs something for you. It‚Äôs something about you,‚ÄĚ said Jason, hurriedly. ‚ÄúI think I‚Äôll walk round the pond.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhy do that?‚ÄĚ protested Wolf. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôll be no secret about it, even if it is for me.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúHe‚Äôll like to find you alone best. These servants of these landowners always do,‚ÄĚ replied the other. ‚ÄúBesides, Mr.¬†Urquhart hates me. He knows I know what he is. He‚Äôs not a common kind of fool. He likes having good meals and good wine, but he‚Äôs ready to risk all that for I don‚Äôt know what!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI tell you I have no secrets with Urquhart,‚ÄĚ rejoined Wolf. ‚ÄúThere‚Äôs absolutely no need for you to leave us.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThis gardener looked at me very suspiciously yesterday,‚ÄĚ whispered Jason. ‚ÄúI saw him through the hedge, in his garden. He was planting something, but he kept looking at the hedge. He must have known I was there. He must have been wondering whether he dared shoot at me with a shotgun. So goodbye! I‚Äôm going to walk round the pond very slowly.‚ÄĚ

Wolf moved toward Mr.¬†Monk, leaving his companion to shuffle off as he pleased. The gigantic servant looked like a respectable prizefighter in his Sunday clothes. When the two men met he took from his pocket a telegram and handed it to Wolf, touching his hat politely as he did so.

‚ÄúThis came early,‚ÄĚ he said. ‚ÄúBut there was no one else to send; and I had to tend to things before I could bring it myself. If there‚Äôs any answer, ‚Äôtwill have to go by way of Blacksod, for our office shuts at noon.‚ÄĚ

Wolf opened the telegram. It was from his mother, and ran as follows:

‚ÄúArrive Ramsgard seven o‚Äôclock Sunday night Tradesmen have no sense Could sleep at Lovelace.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThere‚Äôs no answer, Monk,‚ÄĚ he said gravely; and then, after prodding the ground thoughtfully with his stick, and looking at the figure of Jason Otter, which was now stationary behind a poplar-tree, ‚ÄúThis is from my mother,‚ÄĚ he added. ‚ÄúShe is coming down from town tonight.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúVery nice for you, Sir, I‚Äôm sure,‚ÄĚ murmured the man. ‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTain‚Äôt every gentleman has got a mother.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBut the difficulty is, Monk,‚ÄĚ Wolf went on, ‚Äúthat my mother wants to stay down here. You don‚Äôt happen to know of any cottage or any rooms in a cottage that we could get for a time, do you?‚ÄĚ

Roger Monk looked at him thoughtfully. ‚ÄúNot that I knows of, Sir,‚ÄĚ he began, his gipsy-like eyes wandering from Wolf‚Äôs face to the landscape in front of him, a portion of which landscape included the figure of Mr.¬†Otter, hiding behind the poplar-tree.

‚ÄúThat is to say, Sir, unless by any chance‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ but that ain‚Äôt likely, Sir.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWhat do you mean, Monk?‚ÄĚ enquired the other, eagerly.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTwere only that I myself live lonesome-like in me own place‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ and seeing you‚Äôre helping Squire with his writings‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ and Lenty Cottage be neat set up, I were just thinking‚Ā†‚ÄĒ‚ÄĚ

Wolf swung his stick. ‚ÄúThe very thing!‚ÄĚ he cried excitedly. In a flash his imagination became abnormally active. He visualized this gardener‚Äôs house in all its details. He saw himself, as well as his mother, snugly ensconced there for years and years‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ perhaps for the rest of their lives!

‚ÄúBut we should be a nuisance to you, Monk, even if the Squire were amenable, shouldn‚Äôt we?‚ÄĚ

The man shook his head.

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôll come straight home with you now, Monk, if I may,‚ÄĚ said Wolf impatiently. ‚ÄúWere you going home now?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI was.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell, I‚Äôll just run and tell Mr.¬†Otter; and then I‚Äôll come with you.‚ÄĚ

He left the man standing where they had been talking, and hurried round the edge of the pond. There was something peculiarly appealing to him in the idea of this cottage. How pleasant it would be, he thought, when he and his mother were living together there some five years hence, if he happened to say to her, as he came in to tea from his Sunday walk, with a bunch of primroses in his hand, ‚ÄúI came past Lenty Pond today, Mother, where I first heard about the chance of our settling here!‚ÄĚ

He found Jason sitting on the roots of the poplar, leaning his back against the tree-trunk and holding the tails of his overcoat stretched tightly over his knees, so that he should be entirely concealed from view.

‚ÄúThat man hasn‚Äôt gone,‚ÄĚ was his greeting to Wolf. ‚ÄúHe‚Äôs standing there still.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI know he is, Otter. He‚Äôs brought a telegram for me. My mother‚Äôs coming down tonight. Monk says he doesn‚Äôt see any reason why she and I shouldn‚Äôt take rooms in his cottage.‚ÄĚ

Jason looked up at him from where he sat upon the poplar-root, and the whimsical manner in which he hugged his coattails was accentuated by a smile of hobgoblinish merriment.

‚ÄúYou mean to live in it?‚ÄĚ he remarked. ‚ÄúYou and your mother? I don‚Äôt believe old Urquhart would consider such a thing for a moment! These squires like to show off their servants‚Äô quarters. They like to take their guests round and say: ‚ÄėThat‚Äôs where my head-gardener lives. He works at his garden when he‚Äôs finished with mine! Those are ‚ÄúBoule de neige‚ÄĚ roses!‚Äô But when it comes to honest people lodging in places like that‚Ā†‚ÄĒgoodness! Urquhart wouldn‚Äôt consider it. But you can try. But my advice to you is to be very careful in this matter. You never know what troubles you‚Äôll have when you deal with people like this Monk. But you can try. There! you‚Äôd better go off with him. He‚Äôs peeping and spying at this moment. He‚Äôs thinking I‚Äôm holding you back because of the money you pay us.‚ÄĚ

Wolf shook his head and made a movement to be gone, but the other bent forward a little and whispered up at him: ‚ÄúI‚Äôll walk slowly round the pond; then if he looks back he won‚Äôt think you ought to wait for me.‚ÄĚ

With this complicated and obscure sentence floating on the surface of his mind, Wolf left his companion to his own devices and rejoined Roger Monk.

Not more than twenty minutes‚Äô walking brought them to the gardener‚Äôs cottage. To Wolf‚Äôs great satisfaction the place proved to be quite out of sight of the manor-house on the Ramsgard side of the orchards and the kitchen-gardens. It stood, indeed, in Lenty Lane, a little east of the drive-gates, and turned out to be a solid little cottage, pleasantly coated with white paint, and approached from the lane by a neat gravel-path, on either side of which was a row of carefully whitewashed small round stones. Wolf for some reason didn‚Äôt like the look of those white stones. Once more he regarded Lenty Cottage. The idea of its excessive neatness and tidiness, combined with the idea of its being so long empty except for this one man, troubled his nerves in some odd way. What did it suggest to him? Ah, he had it! It suggested the peculiar lonely trimness‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ so extraordinarily forbidding‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ of a gaoler‚Äôs house outside a prison-gate, or a keeper‚Äôs house outside a lunatic-asylum.

‚ÄúWell, let‚Äôs see the inside,‚ÄĚ he said, turning to his companion. ‚ÄúMr.¬†Urquhart might as well have put me up here at first.‚ÄĚ

The other gave him one of his equivocal glances. ‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTwere the matter of meals, I expect, Sir,‚ÄĚ he said cautiously. ‚ÄúBut if the lady comes, things will be different, no doubt.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúThen you‚Äôd be pleased to have us here?‚ÄĚ

This time the gardener‚Äôs look was direct and eager.

‚ÄúI‚Äôd be glad enough to have a gent like yourself sleeping under this here roof,‚ÄĚ he cried.

They entered the house together and the matter was soon arranged between them. When things were settled, Wolf observed the man rubbing one of his hands up and down the back of a chair. ‚ÄúI‚Äôd give a hundred pounds to get a place in them Shires again!‚ÄĚ he burst out suddenly.

Wolf looked at him in astonishment. ‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt like it here, Monk,‚ÄĚ he murmured.

‚ÄúLike it?‚ÄĚ The man‚Äôs voice sank to a whisper. ‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄôTis easier to enter a gentleman‚Äôs service than to leave it, Sir, when that gentleman be the sort of Nebuchadnezzar my master be!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYou aren‚Äôt a Dorset man, then?‚ÄĚ enquired Wolf.

‚ÄúI were born here,‚ÄĚ replied the other, ‚Äúbut I left home when I were a kiddie, and worked in they Shires.‚ÄĚ

This remark made clear to Wolf a great deal about Roger Monk. The upper layers of the man‚Äôs mind were sophisticated by travel. The deeper ones retained their indigenous imprint.

‚ÄúWell, I must go back to Pond Cottage now,‚ÄĚ Wolf said calmly. ‚ÄúMrs.¬†Otter and Mr.¬†Darnley ought to be back from church by this time, and I must talk to them. We‚Äôll arrange about terms, Monk, after I‚Äôve seen Mr.¬†Urquhart. Do you suppose I should find him at home now, if I looked in on my way to the cottage?‚ÄĚ

A frown of concentrated concern clouded the countenance of the man in front of him.

‚ÄúIt certainly would be best,‚ÄĚ he remarked, ‚Äúif it could be done. What he‚Äôll say to it, I don‚Äôt know, I‚Äôm sure.‚ÄĚ

With these words ringing in his ears, Wolf, some fifteen minutes later, found himself admitted to Mr.¬†Urquhart‚Äôs presence. He discovered his employer in his study, reading with fascinated interest the book which his new secretary had brought him.

‚ÄúThese Evershots will be the making of our history,‚ÄĚ he chuckled, in high glee. ‚ÄúYou did well with old Malakite. Five pounds for this? I tell you, it‚Äôs worth twenty! You‚Äôre a capital ambassador, Mr.¬†Solent!‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ Eh? What‚Äôs that? Your mother coming here?‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ Monk‚Äôs front-rooms?‚ÄĚ

He straightened out his legs and smoothed back his glossy hair from each side of that carefully brushed parting. With his great white face drooping a little on one side, with the flabby folds under his eyelids pulsing as if they possessed an independent life of their own, he made an unpleasant impression on Wolf‚Äôs mind.

Mr.¬†Urquhart‚Äôs study was a small, dingy room, the walls of which were entirely covered by eighteenth-century prints. The Squire sat in a low, leather chair, with the Evershot chronicle on his knees; and as Wolf settled himself opposite him in a similar chair, he began to feel that, after all, he was probably exaggerating the peculiarities of King‚Äôs Barton Manor.

‚ÄúIt‚Äôs my nervous imagination, I expect,‚ÄĚ he said to himself. ‚ÄúUrquhart‚Äôs no doubt like hundreds of other eccentric men of leisure. And as for the gardener‚Äôs chatter‚Ā†‚ÄĒI suppose servants are always glad to grumble to a stranger.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúDidn‚Äôt my predecessor live in Monk‚Äôs house?‚ÄĚ he found himself saying.

The squire lifted his hand from the book he held and half raised it to his well-shaven chin. ‚ÄúRedfern? A little while, perhaps. I really forget. Not long, anyway. That drunken individual at Pond Cottage persuaded him to go to them. It was with them he died. They told you that, I suppose?‚ÄĚ

Mr.¬†Urquhart‚Äôs voice was so placid and casual as he made these remarks that Wolf was seized with a sort of shame for letting his imagination run riot so among all these new acquaintances. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs the difference from London! That‚Äôs what explains it,‚ÄĚ he thought to himself.

Mr.¬†Urquhart now stopped scratching his chin with his delicate fingertips, and, bowing his head a little, fumbled once more with the pages of the book upon his knee. Wolf sank back into his deep armchair and stared at the man‚Äôs tweed trousers and shiny patent-leather shoes.

He drew a long breath that was something between a sigh of weariness and a sigh of relief. His recent interviews with Jason and Monk had given him the feeling of being on the edge of a psychic maelstrom of morbid conflicts. The comfort of this remote room and the ease of this leather chair made him at once weary of agitations and glad that he still could feel like a spectator rather than a combatant.

After all, why should he worry himself? As the philosophical Duke of Albany murmured in King Lear: ‚ÄúThe event! Well‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ The event!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúHow will your mother appreciate sharing her kitchen with my man?‚ÄĚ said Mr.¬†Urquhart suddenly.

The remark irritated Wolf. What did this easy gentleman know about the shifts of poverty? He was himself so bent upon the arrangement that these little matters seemed quite unimportant.

‚ÄúOh, she won‚Äôt mind that!‚ÄĚ he responded carelessly.

‚ÄúWhat put all this into Roger‚Äôs great, stupid, silly head?‚ÄĚ the squire went on, in his silkiest voice. ‚ÄúIs he tired of my company? Does he want to leave my service and enter your mother‚Äôs? What‚Äôs up with the man? It isn‚Äôt the money. I know that much. Roger cares less for money than any man I‚Äôve ever dealt with. What can he be up to now?‚ÄĚ

Wolf remained silent, letting him run on. But in his mind he set himself once more to wonder how far he really had exaggerated the sinister element in his employer‚Äôs character.

But Mr.¬†Urquhart leaned forward now and regarded him intently. ‚ÄúYou won‚Äôt play me a trick, will you, like the other one? But you‚Äôre not tricky, Mr.¬†Solent, I can see that! On my soul, I think you‚Äôre an honest young man. Your face shows it. It has its faults as a face; but it isn‚Äôt tricky.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ Well‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ well‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ well!‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ When does your mother arrive? I shall be interested to have the honour of meeting her again. My cousin Carfax was at one time‚Ā†‚ÄĒyou know, I suppose?‚Ā†‚ÄĒexcessively in love with her.‚Ā†‚Ää‚Ā†‚Ä¶ Not tonight, eh? Well, perhaps that‚Äôs as well. Mrs.¬†Martin shall go over there and make everything straight.‚ÄĚ

Wolf rose to his feet at this point, anxious to take his leave before the man had time to read him any passages from the Evershot Diary. Once outside the house, he took stock of the situation. He had settled matters with the occupier and with the owner of his new abode. The final arrangement he had to make was with Mrs.¬†Otter. Therefore, off he hurried to Pond Cottage, where he found his hostess just returned from church.

But here he met with nothing but sympathy‚Ā†‚ÄĒwhether, in her secret heart she was glad to get rid of him, Wolf could not say. She may have all the while regretted the loss to her eldest son of that chamber whose walls Wolf had so arbitrarily denuded. Well! They could put those pictures back on those walls now! And he mentally resolved to pay as few visits as possible to the bedroom of Mr.¬†Jason Otter. He had no wish to behold the countenance of that ‚Äúgod of rain‚ÄĚ again!

He left Pond Cottage soon after lunch, explaining that he would return that night, but would have supper in Ramsgard with his mother. The afternoon proved to be as misty and warm as the earlier hours of the day; and as he retraced the track of Thursday‚Äôs drive with Darnley, he did not permit the various agitations into which he had been plunged to destroy his delight in that relaxed and caressing weather. He found that travelling on foot in full daylight revealed to him many tokens of the Spring that he had missed on his evening drive.

Once or twice he descended into the ditches on either side of the road, where the limp whitish-pink stalks of half-hidden primroses drooped above their crinkled leaves, and, with hands and knees embedded in the warm-scented earth, pressed his face against those fragile apparitions.

The sweet, faint odour of these pale flowers made him think of Gerda Torp, and he began worrying his mind a good deal as to the effect of his mother‚Äôs arrival upon the progress of his adventure.

Long before he reached the outskirts of Ramsgard he was reminded of his approach to the famous West Country School by the various groups of straw-hatted boys‚Ā†‚ÄĒtall, reserved, disdainful‚Ā†‚ÄĒwho seemed exploring, like young Norman invaders, these humble pasture-lands of the West Saxons.

One or two of the boys, as they passed him by, made hesitating half-gestures of respectful recognition. One of them actually lifted his straw-hat. Wolf became a little embarrassed by these encounters. He wondered what kind of a master these polite neophytes‚Ā†‚ÄĒfor it must have been the newcomers at the place who blundered in this way‚Ā†‚ÄĒmistook him for! Did he look like a teacher of French?
